chapter_summary: Suta recalls how the nectar (amrita) was churned and the divine horse Uccaihshravas arose. Seeing the horse, Kadru asks her sister Vinata its color; Vinata declares it pure white, but Kadru claims its tail is black and proposes a wager in which the loser becomes the other's slave. They agree to settle it the next day. Scheming to win by deceit, Kadru orders her thousand serpent sons to turn into black hairs and cover the horse's tail. When they refuse, she curses them: they will be burned by fire at the snake sacrifice of the royal sage Janamejaya, son of the Pandavas. Brahma (Pitamaha), hearing this cruel fated curse, together with the gods approves it, seeing the serpents are too numerous and too venomous for the welfare of creatures; he then gives the venom-neutralizing knowledge to the great sage Kashyapa.

story.title: The Wager of Kadru and Vinata over Uccaihshravas
story.arc: A sisterly dispute over the color of the divine horse Uccaihshravas escalates into a slavery-stake wager. Kadru, determined to win by deceit, orders her serpent sons to falsify the horse's tail; their refusal earns them her curse of fiery destruction at Janamejaya's future snake sacrifice. Brahma, weighing the serpents' dangerous overpopulation, sanctions the curse yet tempers it by granting Kashyapa the antidote-knowledge, planting the seed of serpent survival.
story.timeline_note: Set in the primordial past just after the churning of the ocean produced Uccaihshravas; it foreshadows the much later snake sacrifice of Janamejaya, the frame within which the whole Mahabharata is recited.
story.comic_potential: Two divine sisters bickering like neighbors over a horse's tail color, then betting their freedom on it; a scheming mother ordering her snake children to literally become hair-extensions, and their flat refusal triggering an apocalyptic curse — absurd domestic drama with cosmic stakes.

teachings.teaching: Deceit pursued to win a wager recoils upon its author and the innocent drawn into it: Kadru's plan to cheat over the horse's tail leads her to curse her own children to death by fire.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, the wager and the scheme are movements of the restless, grasping mind (the dasi/bondage motif): identification with winning enslaves; the curse on one's own 'sons' mirrors how lower compulsions of the psyche destroy the very faculties they were meant to command.

teachings.teaching: A son's duty has a limit at unrighteous command: the serpents refuse Kadru's order to deceive, accepting a curse rather than commit the fraud.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the refusal as the discriminating power (viveka) within the being asserting itself against the lower will's command — even at the cost of suffering, the truth-aligned faculty will not bend to deceit.

teachings.teaching: Divine governance balances justice with mercy and the wider good: Brahma sanctions the destructive curse only because the serpents had become too numerous and too venomous for creatures' welfare, yet simultaneously preserves life by giving Kashyapa the venom-neutralizing knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's terms this is the play of cosmic intelligence that prunes excess while safeguarding continuity — destruction and the antidote arise together, the same wisdom that both withdraws and sustains.
